title: The Return

jokes.text: I am Harvey, a computer. Jim sucks.
jokes.setup: Michael introduces his new computer persona to Jim
jokes.punchline: The computer immediately insults Jim unprovoked
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael obliviousness - using technology inappropriately for childish humor. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, Jim, I'd like you to meet my new computer persona. Computer, say hello to Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Michael (as Computer)
dialogue.line: Hello, Jim. You're a terrible salesman and your hair looks like a bird's nest.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What? Michael, what is this?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I told you, it's my computer persona. He's very honest.

jokes.text: That's so rude. I'm sorry, I can't control him. / Yeah, you can.
jokes.setup: Michael pretends he can't control his computer voice
jokes.punchline: Jim matter-of-factly points out the obvious lie
jokes.explanation: Jim's deadpan response to Michael's absurd premise. Economy is excellent - two words deflate Michael's entire act.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't know what's wrong with this computer. The voice won't stop!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You're literally typing right now.

jokes.text: Pam, you look very hot today.
jokes.setup: Michael calls Pam into the office
jokes.punchline: Immediately makes inappropriate comment via computer voice
jokes.explanation: Predictable Michael inappropriate behavior. Gets the cringe but lacks cleverness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam, can you come in here for a second?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, what's up?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I just want to say... you look very nice today.

dialogue.speaker: Computer Voice
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: Me so horny. Me love you long tim. / Who's Long Tim?
jokes.setup: Michael does offensive Asian stereotype voice
jokes.punchline: Pam misunderstands 'long time' as a person named Long Tim
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - Pam's innocent misunderstanding deflates Michael's offensive joke into absurdity. Perfect character-driven humor.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's been a long time since we've seen each other.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Long Tim? Is he coming to the office?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, Pam, 'long time' — it means it's been a while.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you were talking about someone named Long Tim.

jokes.text: Well, you should bring Long Tim in one day. / I'd love to meet Long Tim.
jokes.setup: Pam continues the Long Tim misunderstanding
jokes.punchline: Jim joins in, treating Long Tim as a real person
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that builds on the previous joke. Jim and Pam's straight-faced commitment sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, and Long Tim is really upset about the new parking situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Long Tim? He's been complaining about that all week. I heard he's thinking about transferring departments.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Right? I mean, Long Tim has been here for years. You'd think they'd consider his opinion.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Exactly. Long Tim deserves better than this.

jokes.text: You ruined a funny joke. Get out of my offfive.
jokes.setup: Michael gets frustrated that his offensive joke backfired
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'office' while trying to be commanding
jokes.explanation: Michael's mispronunciation adds to his petulant childishness. Decent character moment but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said! No, wait, that doesn't work here. Look, the point is, I'm the Regional Manager of this... this... o-FEESH. O-FEESH! You all work in my o-FEESH and you will respect me!

jokes.text: Boobs!
jokes.setup: Michael apparently continues playing with the computer voice after everyone leaves
jokes.punchline: Yells 'Boobs!' into empty office
jokes.explanation: Lazy shock humor. Gets a cheap laugh but no craft. Just Michael being juvenile.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Boobs!

jokes.text: Is it a big deal? / Is it, Kevin? / Do you really not know? / Because it is a big deal.
jokes.setup: Angela explains about missing tax forms
jokes.punchline: Kevin's genuine confusion about whether it's a big deal, Angela's exasperated response
jokes.explanation: Perfect character dynamic - Angela's intensity vs Kevin's obliviousness. Realistic workplace tension.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Kevin, I can't find the Q3 tax forms anywhere. They're just gone.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Is that bad?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Is that bad? Kevin, yes, that's bad. That's very bad. We need those forms.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought maybe you were just being dramatic.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: No, Kevin. This is not dramatic. This is a crisis.

jokes.text: It's like we're touring Willy Wonka's Chocolate Factory and dropping off one by one. Well, guess what. I'm not falling in a chocolate river.
jokes.setup: Jim explains how Stamford transfers are disappearing
jokes.punchline: Extends Wonka metaphor with determination not to 'fall in chocolate river'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant extended metaphor that perfectly captures the situation. Creative, quotable, and shows Jim's wit under pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So the Stamford branch is closing, and everyone's transferring here. It's like we're all going through Willy Wonka's factory, except instead of finding golden tickets, we're just trying not to fall in the chocolate river.

jokes.text: Hey, Dwight, pass the tardy sauce. Get it, Michael?
jokes.setup: Andy tries to make a pun about Dwight being late
jokes.punchline: Obvious 'tardy sauce' instead of 'tartar sauce' pun, then asks if Michael gets it
jokes.explanation: Terrible pun made worse by Andy explaining it. Perfect character moment for try-hard Andy but weak comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dwight, you're late again. I guess you could say you need some tardy sauce with that.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't get it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Tardy sauce. Like tartar sauce. Because you're tardy.

jokes.text: Michael Scott. PhD. Doctor of sales.
jokes.setup: Andy gets to pick his sales partner first
jokes.punchline: Picks Michael and gives him a fake doctorate in sales
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy brown-nosing. The PhD joke is mild but fits his character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I pick Michael as my sales partner.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Really? That's great!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I'm giving you a fake doctorate in sales. You can put it on your wall.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: A doctorate? In sales?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dr. Michael Scott. Has a nice ring to it, doesn't it?

jokes.text: We're the same age.
jokes.setup: Michael calls Phyllis 'our resident senior'
jokes.punchline: Phyllis matter-of-factly corrects that they're the same age
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - three words deflate Michael's condescension. Classic Phyllis quiet assertiveness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Phyllis, our resident senior.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: We're the same age.

jokes.text: Pass. / You can't pass, You gotta pick somebody. / I'll take the kid.
jokes.setup: Stanley is asked to pick a partner
jokes.punchline: Tries to pass, forced to pick, reluctantly takes Ryan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stanley - maximum disinterest and minimal effort. 'The kid' shows he barely knows Ryan's name.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Stanley, you need to pick a partner.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: I pass.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You can't pass, Stanley. Everyone has to pick.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Fine. Ryan.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Don't get excited about it.

jokes.text: I was his second choice, after 'pass'.
jokes.setup: Ryan talking head after being picked by Stanley
jokes.punchline: Points out he was second choice after Stanley tried to avoid picking anyone
jokes.explanation: Great callback with perfect Ryan deadpan. The quotation marks around 'pass' make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: He didn't want to pick anyone. I was his second choice. Which is... I mean, that's not great.

jokes.text: Oh, young Jim. I was just... so much... I need to warn you about, and yet, tragically, I cannot.
jokes.setup: Jim looks at old photo of himself with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Addresses his past self like a time traveler who can't change the future
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor about hindsight. Jim's dramatic delivery makes the mundane situation feel epic.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know what you're thinking. 'How did I end up here?' But I can't tell you. I can't change anything. I'm basically a time traveler who knows exactly how this ends, and there's nothing I can do about it.

jokes.text: Sebring, by Chrysler. A heck of a motor carriage.
jokes.setup: Dwight apparently commenting on something in the photo
jokes.punchline: Calls a car a 'motor carriage' like he's from the 1800s
jokes.explanation: Pure Dwight - anachronistic language that perfectly captures his old-fashioned worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's a fine motor carriage you have there.

jokes.text: You guys are the retired marines. And, you guys are the mother and daughter. And, you guys are the gay couple. And, we are the fire fighter heroes.
jokes.setup: Michael explains the Amazing Race comparison
jokes.punchline: Assigns stereotypical Amazing Race team types, saves 'heroes' for himself and Andy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Michael's self-aggrandizement, but the joke is fairly obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, so I'm dividing everyone up into Amazing Race teams. Ryan and Kelly, you guys are the young couple. Pam and Jim, you're the boyfriend-girlfriend team. Dwight and Angela, you're the old married couple.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And me and Andy? We're the heroes.

jokes.text: Then, how is this amazing race? / It's... just... It's Amazing Race, Phyllis, okay?
jokes.setup: Phyllis questions the logic of calling it Amazing Race
jokes.punchline: Michael gets defensive and can't explain his own metaphor
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael - creates elaborate metaphor then gets flustered when questioned. Phyllis's logic exposes his nonsense.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Why is it called the Amazing Race? I mean, what's so amazing about it?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, because it's... amazing. And there's a race. So Amazing Race.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: But what makes it amazing?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: The amazingness of it, Phyllis. God.

jokes.text: It's the safest part of a car. In the event of a crash, the driver always protects his side first.
jokes.setup: Dwight questions Jim sitting in the back seat
jokes.punchline: Jim gives logical-sounding but absurd reason about driver self-preservation
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim - sounds reasonable but is completely illogical. Perfect deadpan delivery of nonsense.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Why are you sitting in the back seat?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, statistically, you're more likely to die in a head-on collision than a rear-end collision. So I'm just trying to maximize my survival odds.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's not how that works.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Isn't it though?

jokes.text: every success I've ever had, at my job or with 'the lady-folk,' Has come from my ability to slowly, and painfully, wear someone down.
jokes.setup: Jim explains his strategy for taking down Dwight
jokes.punchline: Admits all his success comes from grinding people down, including romantic relationships
jokes.explanation: Dark but honest self-assessment. 'Lady-folk' is a great touch, and the grinding down strategy is genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: My strategy for taking down Dwight is simple: I grind people down. I grind them down until they break. It works in the office, it works in romantic relationships, it works everywhere.

jokes.text: Wow, that is a long story, but quite well told, Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael tells a very brief story about Dwight's betrayal
jokes.punchline: Jim sarcastically compliments the 'long' story that was actually very short
jokes.explanation: Solid sarcasm that plays off Michael's tendency to oversell his stories. Jim's manipulation beginning.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wow, that was a long story.

jokes.text: I used to work at Abercrombie, So... pretty good folder.
jokes.setup: Jim offers to do Michael's laundry better than Dwight
jokes.punchline: Cites Abercrombie experience as qualification for folding clothes
jokes.explanation: Good character detail that feels authentic to Jim's background. Sets up the manipulation plot nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I can fold clothes better than Dwight. I have experience folding clothes at Abercrombie & Fitch.

jokes.text: Why are we turning in here? This is beauty salon.
jokes.setup: Jim and Dwight arrive somewhere for their sales call
jokes.punchline: Dwight's confusion about why they're at a beauty salon
jokes.explanation: Sets up the unconventional sales call location. Dwight's grammar ('This is beauty salon') adds character flavor.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, we're here for the sales call.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A beauty salon? Jim, this is clearly a waste of our time. We're salesmen, not... aestheticians.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, they use paper. That's our product.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that changes things. Dunder Mifflin paper is superior for all salon applications.

jokes.text: Nothing would delight me more!
jokes.setup: Jim asks if he can take the lead on the sales call
jokes.punchline: Dwight's overly enthusiastic agreement to step back
jokes.explanation: Pure Dwight enthusiasm. The formal language and over-the-top eagerness is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Can I take the lead on the sales call?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes. Of course. I am a team player. I will sit back and let you do all the talking. I will be silent. Watch me not speak.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Great.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Not a word will come out of my mouth. I am like a statue. A silent, watchful statue.

jokes.text: Leave the keys. / You still do that thing? / Leave the keys.
jokes.setup: Pam and Roy have a brief interaction
jokes.punchline: Pam's repetition and Roy's question suggest an ongoing relationship dynamic
jokes.explanation: Realistic relationship tension in few words. Shows their dynamic without explanation - very economical writing.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'what'?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'what do you mean what'?

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Why do you keep saying 'what'?

jokes.text: Except for Dwight. He is kind more of a super dud. I mean, he'd be a super friend, if there was a Super Friends who has super power was always being late.
jokes.setup: Jim talks about the team being like Super Friends
jokes.punchline: 'Super dud' pun and the idea of lateness as a superpower
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay with 'super dud.' The lateness-as-superpower concept is mildly clever but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We're like Super Friends. You know, we all have different powers.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam's like the invisible jet, Dwight's like super strength, I'm like super speed...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Stanley's superpower is arriving late. Which makes us Super Dud.

jokes.text: Hawkman.
jokes.setup: Jim continues the Super Friends metaphor
jokes.punchline: Random reference to Hawkman as punctuation
jokes.explanation: Weak ending to the Super Friends bit. Just a random character name without context or payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's like we're the Super Friends, you know? We all have our powers. Pam's got her receptionist superpowers, Ryan's got his... temporary intern powers, and I'm just here being Hawkman.

jokes.text: I have walked two marathons, so...
jokes.setup: Jim explains he doesn't give up easily
jokes.punchline: 'Walked' two marathons instead of ran them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim - sounds impressive until you realize walking a marathon isn't really an achievement. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I don't give up easily. I've 'walked' two marathons instead of ran them.

jokes.text: 7 out of 10 attacks are from the rear. / Okay, but that still leaves a 30 percent chance that I'll attack you from the front.
jokes.setup: Dwight explains why he doesn't let people walk behind him
jokes.punchline: Jim points out this means there's still a 30% chance he'll attack from the front
jokes.explanation: Brilliant logic trap. Jim uses Dwight's own statistics to create a new threat scenario. Perfect character dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't let people walk behind me. It's a power move.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So there's still a 30% chance you'll attack from the front?

jokes.text: I caught an 80-pound shark off Montauk. It's in Hamtons. My dad's got a 42-foot Bayliner. Sniped it with a rifle from the crow's nest.
jokes.setup: Casual fishing conversation
jokes.punchline: Andy's increasingly absurd one-upmanship, culminating in sniping a shark with a rifle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy escalation. Each detail gets more absurd, ending with the ridiculous shark sniping. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So I was out fishing last weekend, caught this massive largemouth bass.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? I caught two massive largemouth bass. Back to back.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And then a pike. A really angry pike.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I didn't even use a rod. I used my bare hands.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Then I waded out into the ocean, and I sniped a shark. With a rifle. From like, a hundred yards away.

jokes.text: We don't work out of a log cabin. We trade on the New York Stock Exchange. Ever heard of it? It's in New York.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to undersell their company size
jokes.punchline: Andy brags about stock exchange, then condescendingly explains it's in New York
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - the condescending 'It's in New York' shows his oblivious superiority complex.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We're a small company. Very small. Basically a startup.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, we're on the stock exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The New York Stock Exchange. It's in New York.

jokes.text: No, I swear, this guy could sell paper to a tree. / Stop it, stop it.
jokes.setup: Andy compliments Michael's sales skills
jokes.punchline: Absurd metaphor about selling paper to trees, Michael's embarrassed reaction
jokes.explanation: Creative twist on 'sell ice to an Eskimo' cliché. The tree-paper connection is clever, even if overdone by Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Your sales skills are incredible, Michael. It's like you could sell paper to trees.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's... that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know, right? That's how good you are.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, I mean trees don't buy paper. They ARE paper.

jokes.text: Oh man, talk about your classic lame-dash-o.
jokes.setup: Andy commenting on the client after a failed sales pitch
jokes.punchline: Makes up the phrase 'lame-dash-o' as if it's a real term
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy - creates fake slang terms to sound cool. 'Lame-dash-o' is appropriately awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That guy was lame-dash-o.

jokes.text: This friend of mine? let's call her, Noelle. She missed this deadline... But then this gallant gentleman? We'll call him Kurt. he drove all the way to New York and handed it in for her!
jokes.setup: Angela tells a story about 'friends' named Noelle and Kurt
jokes.punchline: Obviously talking about herself and Dwight but using fake names
jokes.explanation: Classic Angela indirection. The fake names and formal language perfectly capture her character. Builds mystery.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I have a friend named Noelle and her boyfriend Kurt. Noelle is very organized and keeps a very tight ship. Kurt is very traditional and hardworking. Well, one day Noelle found out that Kurt had been sneaking around behind her back.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: And you know what the worst part was? It wasn't the betrayal. It was that he did it with someone so beneath him. Someone so... common.

jokes.text: I really Schruted it. / What? / Schruted it. This is the thing that people say in your office all the time. Like when you screw something up in a really irreversible way, You Schruted it.
jokes.setup: Andy apologizes for messing up the sales call
jokes.punchline: Claims 'Schruted it' is common office slang for screwing up badly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant invention that feels completely plausible. Perfect Andy manipulation while creating quotable catchphrase. Exceptional character work.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm sorry about that sales call. I really Schruted it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, what does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, Schruted it. It's office slang. For when you mess something up really badly.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I've never heard that before.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it's pretty common around here.

jokes.text: I don't know where the word comes from, though. I think it comes from Dwight Schrute. I don't know. Who knows how words are formed?
jokes.setup: Andy continues explaining 'Schruted it'
jokes.punchline: Pretends not to know the obvious etymology while explaining it, ends with philosophical deflection
jokes.explanation: Excellent follow-up that sells the lie through fake uncertainty. 'Who knows how words are formed?' is perfect pseudo-intellectual Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So 'Schruted it' comes from... well, nobody really knows. Some say it's from an old German word, 'schruten,' meaning to mess something up. Others think it's onomatopoeia—the sound failure makes. But honestly, does the etymology really matter? What matters is that we all understand what it means when someone says they Schruted it. And isn't that what language is really about?

jokes.text: Um... Hi. Hello, Ryan. What do you have for us?
jokes.setup: Ryan is supposed to give a sales pitch while Stanley does crosswords
jokes.punchline: Ryan's complete inability to start, just stammering
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ryan incompetence. The long pause and stammering show his complete unpreparedness.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Um... so... I... uh... well, you see, the thing is... um...

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Are you going to sell something or...

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Yes! No, I mean... I was just... there's this... um... product and it's... it's really...

jokes.text: And you just said, 'Hi. Hi. Hi.' You sounded like my niece. And she's six months old!
jokes.setup: Stanley critiques Ryan's terrible sales attempt
jokes.punchline: Compares Ryan's eloquence to a six-month-old baby
jokes.explanation: Brutal but accurate assessment from Stanley. The baby comparison is harsh but perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Ryan, I've seen a six-month-old baby with better sales skills than that.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: At least the baby has an excuse for not being able to talk.

jokes.text: Dunder Mifflin customer service, this is Kelly. / Oh my god, Jim, how are you?
jokes.setup: Jim calls Dunder Mifflin to demonstrate customer service
jokes.punchline: Kelly immediately recognizes him and gets personal instead of professional
jokes.explanation: Great setup that immediately backfires. Kelly's unprofessional enthusiasm undermines Jim's demo perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm calling to speak with someone about your customer service.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Jim! Hi! How are you? I haven't talked to you in forever! What's going on? Are you still with Pam? You guys are so cute together.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're good. But I'm actually calling as a potential customer—

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: That's so great! So what do you need? Because I can totally help you. Do you want to hear about our new paper lines? They're amazing. Oh, and did you hear that Ryan got promoted? Again. I know, right?

jokes.text: I never take vacations, I never get sick, and I don't celebrate any major holidays.
jokes.setup: Jim promises dedicated customer service
jokes.punchline: Lists increasingly sad personal sacrifices, especially the holidays
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of dedication that becomes pathetic. The holidays line adds the right touch of sadness.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I promise you dedicated customer service. I will be available 24/7. I'll skip lunch. I'll skip dinner. I'll skip sleep. I'll skip my son's birthday. I'll skip my daughter's recital. I'll skip Thanksgiving. I'll skip Christmas. I'll skip my own funeral.

jokes.text: He was hung up on Pam for such a long time... I didn't think he'd ever get over her.
jokes.setup: Kelly talks to Karen about how nice she is
jokes.punchline: Casually drops that Jim was hung up on Pam, creating awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly - completely oblivious to creating awkward situation. Advances plot while staying in character.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: You're like, so nice. I don't know how you do it.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Thanks, Kelly. I just try to be kind to everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: That's so cool. Oh, by the way, Jim was like, totally hung up on Pam for the longest time.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: You can pay me back later for the makeover.
jokes.setup: Jim presumably reacts to the awkward Kelly revelation
jokes.punchline: Tries to deflect with joke about paying for makeover
jokes.explanation: Weak deflection attempt from Jim. The joke doesn't quite land but shows his discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So... Kelly got a makeover?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'd pay for that too if someone told me I needed one.

jokes.text: Why would Dwight go New York without telling anyone? / You think he went to see Jan?
jokes.setup: Andy shows Michael evidence of Dwight's NYC trip
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately assumes the worst - that Dwight went to Jan
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Michael's paranoia. His immediate jump to Jan shows his insecurity about past betrayals.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Michael, look at this. Dwight was in New York City last weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: New York? Dwight went to see Jan.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? No, he was probably just—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: He went to see Jan. I know it.

jokes.text: Fool me once, strike one. But fool me twice... strike three.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to quote the 'fool me twice' saying
jokes.punchline: Completely mangles it by mixing it with baseball terminology
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael malapropism. Perfect confusion of two different sayings that creates something absurd but quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Fool me once, strike one. Fool me twice, strike three.

jokes.text: She could be a model. Or a college professor. Which is intimidating to a lot of guys.
jokes.setup: Dwight talks about his girlfriend being beautiful and smart
jokes.punchline: Thinks being a college professor is as intimidating as being a model
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight logic - equating intellectual and physical intimidation. Shows his unique worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My girlfriend is beautiful and smart.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Being a college professor is just as intimidating as being a model.

jokes.text: And where it asks you to state your business, he wrote, 'Beeswax Not Yours, Inc.'
jokes.setup: Andy investigates Dwight's visit to corporate
jokes.punchline: Dwight wrote 'Beeswax Not Yours, Inc.' instead of his actual business
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Dwight humor - transforms 'none of your beeswax' into fake company name. Creative and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dwight, what were you doing at corporate?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I was presenting my new business idea.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What business idea?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Beeswax Not Yours, Inc.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Beeswax... not yours? Dwight, that's not even a real business.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It is a real business. I sell beeswax products, and it's none of your business.

jokes.text: That's what she said. / Don't you dare.
jokes.setup: Michael gives Dwight ultimatum about thinking long and hard
jokes.punchline: Dwight delivers Michael's signature line, Michael forbids it
jokes.explanation: Perfect role reversal - Dwight uses Michael's catchphrase against him. Michael's desperate 'Don't you dare' shows his powerlessness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You need to think long and hard about what you're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No. No, you can't do that. That's my thing.

jokes.text: I hate those two people more than anything in the entire world.
jokes.setup: Dwight suggests they could be like Kelly and Ryan
jokes.punchline: Angela's extreme hatred for the couple they're being compared to
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela venom. The extreme language ('more than anything in the entire world') is hilariously over-the-top but in character.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Kelly and Ryan? Are you insane? Kelly is a vapid, manipulative nightmare, and Ryan is a spineless idiot who got her pregnant. I would rather eat glass than be compared to those two.

jokes.text: Did you ever have a thing for Pam? / Pam? Did I ever have a thing for her? No... Why, did she say something?
jokes.setup: Karen directly asks Jim about Pam
jokes.punchline: Jim's obvious lies and nervous deflection asking if Pam said something
jokes.explanation: Good uncomfortable moment. Jim's transparent lying and nervous question show his guilt perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Karen
dialogue.line: Did Pam say something?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What? No. Why would she say something? Did she say something?

jokes.text: Although, I've loved this company more than almost anything in the world, I decided to step down from my post and spend more time with my family.
jokes.setup: Dwight gives formal resignation speech
jokes.punchline: Uses clichéd politician resignation language for office job
jokes.explanation: Good parody of political resignation speeches. Dwight's formality for a paper sales job is appropriately absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I hereby tender my resignation, effective immediately. I have decided to spend more time with my family and pursue other interests. It has been an honor to serve the Dunder Mifflin Scranton branch, and I am proud of the legacy I leave behind.

jokes.text: But remember... while today it is me, we all shall fall.
jokes.setup: Dwight continues his dramatic resignation
jokes.punchline: Ends with ominous biblical-style warning about everyone's eventual downfall
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight grandiosity. The biblical tone and dramatic fatalism perfectly capture his character.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You all laughed at me. You mocked my beet farm, my martial arts training, my unwavering loyalty to this company. But mark my words—the reckoning is coming. I have seen the future, and it is dark. Your sales numbers will plummet. Your coffee will grow cold. And when the time is right, I shall return to reclaim what is rightfully mine. Repent, for the day of Schrute is upon you.

jokes.text: Oompa loompa, doompadee dossum, Dwight is now gone, which is totally awesome. Why was he gone, he was such a nice guy. No, he was not, he was a total douche. Doompadee doom.
jokes.setup: Kelly improvises Oompa Loompa song about Dwight leaving
jokes.punchline: Contradicts herself mid-song, calling him nice then a douche
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to Jim's Wonka reference. Kelly's stream-of-consciousness contradiction is perfect for her character.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: ♪ Dwight, Dwight, he's leaving us today, he's such a nice guy in every way... ♪

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: ♪ ...what a total douche, what a total douche... ♪

jokes.text: Dwight will be missed. Not by me ... so much, but he will be missed.
jokes.setup: Michael gives eulogy-style speech about Dwight
jokes.punchline: Immediately undercuts sentiment by admitting he won't miss him
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael contradiction. Sets up fake sentiment then immediately deflates it with honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight was an incredible friend, a loyal colleague, and honestly one of the best people I've ever known. I'm going to miss him so much.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Actually, no I won't. I won't miss him at all.

jokes.text: One of my life goals was to die right here, in my desk chair. And today, that dream was shattered.
jokes.setup: Dwight talks about his career goals
jokes.punchline: His life goal was literally to die at his desk, now shattered by leaving
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark Dwight humor. The idea of dying at your desk as a life goal is both absurd and tragically believable for him.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My life goal was literally to die at my desk. Now that's shattered by leaving.

jokes.text: Well, happy's such an ugly word.
jokes.setup: Jim is asked if he's happy about how things turned out
jokes.punchline: Calls happiness 'ugly' while clearly being pleased with the outcome
jokes.explanation: Good Jim moment showing his guilt about engineering Dwight's departure. The contradiction reveals his conflicted feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Happiness? That's ugly. But yeah, I'm pretty happy about how things turned out.

tmdb_overview: Since Dwight's departure, Andy had been clinging to Michael. Andy's antics had been wearing very thin on his co-workers. In fact, Andy left Jim actually wishing Dwight would come back.
insight: Cringe comedy carries a middling 78.9 score on character humor alone.
